qas.q: What were Military Police companies in the new Army Air Force named?
qas.a: Army Air Force Base Security Battalions.

qas.q: What did General Order No. 1, 2 Jan 1948, accomplish?
qas.a: Military Police units serving in the Army Air Corps before separation were transferred to the Air Force and those units and individuals serving in them were designated "Air Police."

qas.q: What were the two main mission elements of "Security Police"?
qas.a: Police and security functions.

qas.q:
What career fields were combined in 1997 to form the "Security Forces"
3P0X1 AFSC?
qas.a:
Security Specialist (811X0), Law Enforcement Specialist to include
Military Working Dog Handler (811X2 and 811X2A), Combat Arms
Training and Maintenance, or CATM (753X0).

qas.q: What Korean War experiences led to the establishment of the "Air Base Defense School" at Parks AFB, California?
qas.a: American and South Korean forces were ill prepared and were forced to fall back hurriedly in the face of the communist onslaught. Air bases in forward areas found themselves suddenly overrun by the enemy. In many instances, Air Police were the only armed force on the base available to provide defenses.

qas.q: Why was the "Whole Base" protective system needed during the Vietnam Conflict?
qas.a: A "whole base" protective system was needed, rather than one centered solely on weapons systems. There were no front lines, no clearly defined battle zones, and no safe "rear areas.

qas.q: What was the key career field reaction to the Khobar Towers Bombing?
qas.a:
The key reaction was the restructure of security police and the
consolidation of three distinct career fields, law enforcement, security,
and combat arms specialties. They all became one specialty in Oct
1997, hereafter collectively being known as "Security Forces."

qas.q:
What important career field milestone resulted in formal training for
"Security Police"?
qas.a:
In March 1971, the enlisted career field was divided into two separate
specialties, security and law enforcement. Prior to this time, graduates
from basic military training could be assigned to SP units without any
technical training. After the split of the career field, security police were
designated as a "Category A" career field, which required formal training.

qas.q: What is the mission of all SF members?
qas.a: The mission of all SF members is to provide a fit-to-fight force protection capability in any location and in any situation.

qas.q: When performing law enforcement functions, why is retaining the word "Police" important?
qas.a: Because it more accurately reflects the uniqueness of the law enforcement mission, and reinforces the bond with fellow police forces the world over.

qas.q: When and to whom was the first Air Police shield issued?
qas.a: In 1959, Vice Chief of Staff, USAF, General Curtis E. LeMay, issued the first official AP Shield to Air Provost Marshall, Brig Gen R.F. Burnham.

qas.q: When was the current SF shield adopted?
Why do SF members wear distinctive uniform items?
qas.a:
Personnel need to readily identify SF members in crisis situations as a symbol of authority, trust and responsibility. Their unique appearance has the effect of providing a deterrent to those who may seek to violate the law, damage and steal property, or harm others. More recently
however, the distinctive uniform, specifically the beret with the Defensor Fortis flash, identifies SF members as an elite group of Air Force personnel charged with the primary responsibility of protecting the Air Force on the ground.

qas.q: What SF uniform item is most symbolic of the SF mission?
qas.a: The SF beret.

qas.q:
What agency publishes the SF Career Field education and Training
Plan?
qas.a: The Air Force Security Forces Center.

qas.q:
SF members must possess what skill level prior to entering the MWD
handler apprentice course?
qas.a: 5-skill level (journeyman).

qas.q:
What AFSC is awarded upon completion of the Combat Arms apprentice
course?
What are the training requirements for awarding of the 3P051 skill level?
qas.a:
Knowledge of the following is mandatory: SF weaponry, laws, directives, programs and policies; and procedures governing SF activities, including installation security, weapon system and resource security,
antiterrorism, law enforcement, investigations, MWD functions, air base defense, armament and equipment, training, pass and registration, information security, corrections, and combat arms.

qas.q: What are the requirements to attend Airman Leadership School?
qas.a: Must be a SSgt select, or SrA with 48 months of total active federal military service.

qas.q: What must be completed prior to assuming the rank of MSgt?
qas.a: Completion of the NCO Academy.

qas.q: Who is the Code of Conduct applicable to?
qas.a: The Code of Conduct is applicable to all military personnel regardless of duty position or branch of service.

qas.q:
Which article of the Code of Conduct would you violate if you gave
information harmful to your fellow prisoners?
qas.a: Article IV.

qas.q: As a POW what information are you required to give when questioned?
qas.a: Your name, rank, service number and date of birth.

qas.q:
What are your actions according to general order number 2, if you
cannot contact a superior?
qas.a: If SF members cannot contact superiors, they must exercise discretion and act according to their training, best judgment, and common sense.

qas.q:
What general conduct guideline would you violate if you consumed
alcohol within 8 hours prior to duty?
qas.a: Attention to duty.

qas.q: What are your guidelines for exercising authority?
qas.a: As on-duty security forces, you are the visible representatives of the US Government, the Air Force, the installation commander, and the chief of security forces. It is your duty to accept the authority entrusted to you and to carry out this important trust impartially, firmly, and in a manner that commands respect from the public.

qas.q: SF squadrons are organized based on what?
qas.a: Mission requirements.

qas.q:
What Command Section position is manned by the senior SF enlisted
leader?
qas.a: Security Forces Manager.

qas.q: What is the First Sergeant responsible for?
qas.a: Serves as the liaison between the commander and the enlisted force. Ensures the commander's policies are known and understood. Manages unit personnel programs.

qas.q:
When enlisted member are in charge of flights what are they
designated?
qas.a: Flight Chiefs.

qas.q: DFC relies on what four key functions? What do the functions do?
qas.a:
S-1 is responsible for: All the unit administrative and personnel functions. These functions are normally associated with the unit commanders support staff in peacetime operations, many continue during wartime.

S-2 is responsible for: All intelligence functions. Millitary decision making in combat requires a continuous process of analyzing the threat and environment in a specific geographic area. Commanders can selectively apply and maximize combat power at critical points in time and space on the battlefield by determining the enemy's likely course of action. Determining that probable course of action is made possible by the collection of intelligence.

S-3 is responsible for: Applying personnel and resources in the most effective places at the most effective times. The S3 can be compared to the operations officer position during base defense operations.

S-4 is responsible for: The S4 function is tasked to supply and re-supply during combat operations. The S4 is responsible for ensuring SF members have the tools needed to perform the wartime mission, to include ammunition, communications equipment, food, vehicles, etc.

qas.q: What are the sector commander duties?
qas.a:
Sector commanders lead SF members in the defense of an assigned sector. They coordinate with adjoining sector commanders on mutual defense issues and report directly to the S3. They ensure the assigned mission is carried out and the needs of their sector are known by the various "S" functions. They are responsible for ensuring all reporting is
accomplished and the routines of defense are carried out and maintained within their assigned sector.
